description: Supports operation as a PCIe Root Complex managing downstream endpoints. Mode selection is controlled via device-level DEVSTAT PCIESSMODE bits. Initialization includes enabling power/clock, configuring SerDes PLL, and controlling LTSSM. Software performs bus enumeration and configuration of downstream ports. This capability enables the SoC to act as the host on the PCIe fabric.
evidence: Section 2.11.1 PCIe as Root Complex; DEVSTAT[PCIESSMODE]; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
dependencies: Reference clock, PCIe power domain on, DEVSTAT, SerDes PLL lock
Remarks: Follow the documented initialization sequence and verify LTSSM state reaches 0x11 before enumeration.

description: Operates as a PCIe Endpoint that responds to configuration, memory, and completion transactions. As an endpoint it is a target for configuration accesses. Software must coordinate with a Root Complex for enumeration and enabling bus mastering. LTSSM enable is required to train the link. This enables integrating the device as a peripheral in a PCIe system.
evidence: Section 2.11.2 and 2.11.2.2 Configuration Accesses; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
dependencies: Reference clock, PCIe power domain, SerDes PLL lock, RC support
Remarks: Ensure BARs and capabilities are programmed before the RC enables bus mastering.

description: The PCIe SerDes provides at least two lanes, allowing configuration per lane. Documentation references configuring Lane 0 and Lane 1 and testing in two-lane mode. Lane-specific settings include RX loss-of-signal and loopback configuration. Proper lane configuration is required prior to starting link training. This enables flexible physical link bring-up and test.
evidence: Section 2.11.1.1 notes and 2-32: SerDes Configuration Lane 0/1; PL_FORCE_LINK usage
dependencies: SerDes reference clock, PCIE_SERDES_CFG* registers, lane config
Remarks: Configure both lanes consistently when operating with two lanes and validate equalization and polarity.

description: Supports automatic polarity inversion on the receive path. This allows successful link training even if differential pair polarity is inverted on the PCB. The feature is part of the physical layer capabilities. No additional glue logic is required. Use it to increase routing flexibility without signal swaps.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Polarity inversion on receive
Remarks: Keep disabled unless board routing requires inversion; verify with compliance tests.

description: Implements a single virtual channel for flow control. This simplifies resource allocation and arbitration. Multi-VC is not available, avoiding VC negotiation complexity. Traffic flows through the default VC. Software should not attempt to enable additional VCs.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Single virtual channel (VC)
Remarks: Design QoS around a single VC and prioritize via traffic class only.

description: Provides a single traffic class for prioritization. This limits QoS granularity but simplifies scheduling. Packets are tagged to the single TC. There is no hardware support to map traffic into multiple TCs. System-level QoS must be handled by software or fabric topology.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Single traffic class (TC)
Remarks: Avoid software attempts to configure additional TCs; not supported.

description: As an Endpoint, the controller exposes a single PCI function. Multi-function endpoint configurations are not supported. This reduces configuration space complexity. Drivers should not enumerate additional functions. Use BARs and capabilities of the single function for resource mapping.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Single function in End Point (EP) mode
Remarks: Keep device/function numbers consistent with single-function expectations.

description: Implements automatic credit management for posted, non-posted, and completion buffers. This reduces software overhead managing flow-control credits. The controller tracks and advertises credits per PCIe protocol. Proper credit handling improves throughput and avoids deadlocks. No application intervention is needed for routine credit updates.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Automatic credit management
Remarks: Tune Max Payload Size and MRRS in config space to balance performance and completion pressure.

description: Supports End-to-End CRC generation on transmitted TLPs and checking on received TLPs. This improves data integrity across the PCIe fabric. Errors detected via ECRC are reported through the error reporting mechanism. Enablement is done through configuration registers. Use this to detect corruption beyond link-level CRC.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: ECRC generation and checking
Remarks: Enable only when both ends support ECRC to avoid false errors.

feature_name: Device power management (no D3 cold with Vaux)
description: Implements PCI device power management states with the exception of D3 cold with auxiliary power. Supports transitions coordinated through config space PM capabilities. Some deep power-down states require power-on reset to exit. Careful sequencing is required when exiting low-power states. D3 cold with Vaux retention is explicitly not supported.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: PCI device power management...; Section 2.13 Reset Considerations
dependencies: Device PM capability, system power rails
Remarks: Avoid designs that assume auxiliary power can retain PCIe state across D3 cold.

description: Supports Active State Power Management states L0s and L1. These reduce power during idle periods while maintaining link. L2 is not supported as a link state. ASPM settings must be coordinated with the link partner. Tuning may impact latency and wake time.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: ASPM state L0s and L1; not supporting L2
dependencies: ASPM control in config space, link partner support
Remarks: Enable only when platform clocking and latency budgets allow; validate with traffic patterns.

description: Implements link-level power management states except L2. L0/L0s/L1 transitions are handled per PCIe spec. L2 is explicitly not implemented. This affects deepest link quiescent state availability. System suspend strategies must not rely on L2.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: PCI Express link power management states, except L2 state
Remarks: Plan platform sleep states around L1, not L2.

description: Provides PCIe Advanced Error Reporting capability. Supports reporting of correctable and uncorrectable errors. Error status and masks are accessible via config space. Proper AER handling enables robust recovery strategies. Integrate with system error logging and interrupts.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: PCI Express advanced error reporting; Tables around 3-73
dependencies: Config space AER capability, interrupt routing
Remarks: Register an AER handler to decode and clear errors promptly.

description: Supports transmission and reception of PCIe Message TLPs. This includes standard messages defined by the spec. Vendor-defined messaging is not supported. Messaging can be used for power, error, and interrupt semantics. Ensure message routing is enabled by the RC.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: PCI Express messages for both transmit and receive
dependencies: Link trained, config space message controls
Remarks: Do not use vendor messages; only standard messages are supported.

description: Implements filtering and handling for posted, non-posted, and completion traffic classes. This assists in appropriate routing and completion lookup. Proper filtering avoids protocol violations. Timeouts on completion lookup can be configured. Use this to protect against stalled transactions.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Filtering for posted, non-posted, and completion traffic; completion lookup/timeout
dependencies: Configuration of completion timeout and filters
Remarks: Set conservative timeouts initially; tune based on platform behavior.

description: Supports configurable Base Address Register filtering, I/O filtering, and configuration filtering. These control inbound access windows and permissions. BAR masks allow flexible sizing. Configuration filtering restricts which config cycles are accepted. Completion lookup/timeout parameters can be tuned.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Configurable BAR filtering, I/O filtering, configuration filtering
dependencies: BAR0/BAR1 and MASK registers, filter controls
Remarks: Ensure BAR alignment and mask settings match system address map.

feature_name: Config/access via BAR0 and config space
description: Provides access to controller configuration space and external application MMIO through BAR0 and configuration cycles. The configuration space has a 1 KB register map with fixed offsets regardless of access mode. This simplifies driver development across RC/EP modes. Software can use BAR0 for application register exposure to the host. Offsets remain constant for predictable programming.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Access to configuration space... through BAR0; Section 2-19: 1 KB configuration space with fixed offsets
dependencies: BAR0 implemented and mapped by RC, config access enabled
Remarks: Reserve BAR0 size per mask and expose only required application registers.

description: Supports legacy interrupt signaling. In RC mode, legacy interrupts can be received. In EP mode, the endpoint can generate legacy assert/deassert messages. Register interfaces include EP_IRQ_SET/CLR/STATUS. Use legacy only when MSI/MSI-X is unavailable.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: Legacy interrupts reception (RC) and generation (EP); Registers: EP_IRQ_SET/EP_IRQ_CLR/EP_IRQ_STATUS (3.1.18–3.1.20)
dependencies: Interrupt controller routing, EP/RC role configured
Remarks: Prefer MSI for scalability; fall back to INTx for compatibility.

description: Supports Message Signaled Interrupts both generating and receiving. Multiple vectors are supported via grouped MSIx-like banks of status and enable registers. Status, enable set/clear, and raw status are provided per group. Vectors map across MSI0..MSI7 groups. This enables finer-grained interrupt handling.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: MSI generation and reception; Registers: MSI0..MSI7 IRQ STATUS/ENABLE (3.1.24–3.1.55); e.g., 3.1.48–3.1.51
dependencies: MSI enabled in config space, MSI IRQ registers configured
Remarks: Program vector enables before arming producers; clear W1C status after servicing.

description: Provides PHY loopback capability for validation in RC mode. This allows internal routing of transmitted data to the receiver for test. Setup requires SerDes configuration and LTSSM state control. Forcing link states can aid loopback bring-up. Use to verify signal integrity and controller framing without a partner.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features: PHY loopback in RC mode; Section 2-32 PL_FORCE_LINK loopback steps
Remarks: Use only in controlled test; restore normal operation after diagnostics.

description: Link Training and Status State Machine can be enabled/disabled via CMD_STATUS. The current LTSSM state is observable in the DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE] field. Disabling training allows safe reconfiguration. Software should assert LTSSM_EN only after SerDes PLL lock. Monitoring state progression confirms successful training.
evidence: Section 2.11 steps 6–9: CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN], DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]
dependencies: SerDes PLL lock, CMD_STATUS, DEBUG0 register
Remarks: Gate training until all configuration is complete; log LTSSM sequence during bring-up.

description: A Port Force Link register allows skipping Detect and forcing specific LTSSM states. This is used for test modes such as PHY loopback. Fields include LINK_STATE and FORCE_LINK. Proper use requires caution to avoid violating protocol timing. It is a diagnostic aid, not for normal operation.
evidence: Section 2-32: PL_FORCE_LINK[LINK_STATE, FORCE_LINK]
dependencies: PL_FORCE_LINK register access, LTSSM disabled/enabled sequence
Remarks: Revert FORCE_LINK to normal after testing to restore autonomous training.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock detect
description: SerDes PLL is configured via PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and status is read via PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]. Reference clock quality and routing through LJCB are discussed. PLL lock occurs within a bounded number of ref cycles when refclkp/n are stable. Proper PLL bandwidth selection depends on clock source. Software must wait for LOCK before enabling LTSSM.
evidence: Section 2.3 SerDes Configuration; 2-5 to 2-7 PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]
dependencies: Stable reference clock, correct PLL bandwidth, power domain on
Remarks: Choose PLL bandwidth per clocking topology; sample LOCK before proceeding.

description: Supports power-on reset, system hard/soft reset, and software-initiated hot reset. Software hot reset is triggered via RSTCMD[INIT_RST] and propagates downstream from RC. After reset, registers return to default except sticky bits. Timing requirements mandate delays before retrying transactions. Functional level reset is not supported.
evidence: Section 2.13 Reset Considerations; RSTCMD[INIT_RST]
dependencies: RC control for downstream hot reset, system reset controller
Remarks: Honor the 100 ms minimum and up to 1.5 s timeout guidance after reset before giving up.

description: The PCIe module requires its power and clock domains to be enabled. Device-level registers include PCIESSEN to enable the module. Power state transitions may require power-on reset to exit certain low-power states. Coordination with the device PSC is necessary. Enablement precedes any configuration or link training.
evidence: Section 2.11 step 2: power and clock domain; DEVSTAT PCIESSEN; Section 2.13.3
Remarks: Sequence power before PLL setup; document dependencies in board init code.

description: An application retry mechanism can be enabled via APP_RETRY_EN to force Retry responses. This allows slow devices additional time during configuration. Software should deassert it once programming completes. It applies to inbound accesses while the device is not yet ready. Use it to avoid premature enumeration failures.
evidence: Section 2.11 step 10: CMD_STATUS[APP_RETRY_EN]
Remarks: Only enable during critical configuration windows; disable for normal operation.

description: Implements BAR0 and BAR1 with mask registers for sizing. A 64-bit BAR0 can be formed using BAR1 as the upper 32 bits. Proper programming determines the memory window exposed. Masks define implemented address bits. This is central to mapping device MMIO into the host address space.
evidence: Section 3.4.3–3.4.8: BAR0, BAR1, BAR0_MASK, BAR1_MASK (64-bit BAR0)
dependencies: RC config cycles to assign addresses, BAR mask logic
Remarks: Align BAR sizes to power-of-two boundaries and verify address decode with tests.

description: Provides registers for bus number, secondary status, and memory base/limit. These assist in hierarchical addressing when acting as a bridge/root port. Proper setup defines upstream and downstream windows. Incorrect values can block traffic. Use documented fields to constrain accessible ranges.
evidence: Section 3.4.9–3.4.11: BUSNUM, SECSTAT, MEMSPACE
Remarks: Program windows after enumeration to prevent unintended accesses.

description: Exposes four general-purpose registers for application use. These can carry software-defined flags or diagnostic data. They are accessible via the application register space. Reads and writes have no side effects beyond storage. Use them for lightweight inter-domain signaling.
evidence: Section 3.1.20–3.1.23: GPR0–GPR3
Remarks: Document any conventions to avoid conflicts between firmware and drivers.

description: MSI vectors are managed via grouped registers with raw status, enabled status, and enable set/clear. Each group maps to specific vector IDs (e.g., MSI6 covers 6,14,22,30). Write-1-to-set and write-1-to-clear semantics apply. This structure simplifies enabling specific vectors. It supports fine-grained interrupt distribution.
evidence: Section 3.1.24–3.1.55: MSI0..MSI7 RAW/STATUS/ENABLE; e.g., Tables 3-48 to 3-51
dependencies: MSI enabled in config space, proper vector routing
Remarks: Map vectors to CPU cores or mailboxes per system interrupt design.

description: Legacy interrupt handling includes A and B channels with raw status, enabled status, and enable set/clear registers. These provide visibility and control for INTx signaling. Use write-1-to-clear to acknowledge. Enable bits gate interrupt delivery. Prefer MSI when available for performance.
evidence: Section 3.1.56–3.1.61: LEGACY_A/B IRQ STATUS/ENABLE
Remarks: Throttle legacy interrupts to avoid line-level storming in high-traffic scenarios.

feature_name: Not supported: split x2 into two x1 links
description: The controller cannot bifurcate a x2 link into two independent x1 links. Hardware exposes the multi-lane link as a single entity. Attempting lane bifurcation is unsupported. System designs must not rely on independent lane operation. Keep routing consistent with unified link behavior.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for using ×2 link as two ×1 links
Remarks: Plan platform slot allocation without expecting lane bifurcation capabilities.

description: Only a single VC is implemented and multiple VCs are not supported. VC negotiation beyond VC0 is unavailable. QoS must be implemented without VC partitioning. This reduces complexity but limits isolation. Software should not enable additional VC capabilities.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for multiple VCs
Remarks: Use TC and software scheduling for QoS instead of VC separation.

description: Only a single traffic class is supported and additional TCs are not available. TC-based prioritization is limited. Any attempt to configure multiple TCs will fail. Plan performance without multi-TC differentiation. This affects latency-sensitive traffic segregation.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for multiple TCs
Remarks: Use alternate mechanisms such as DMA priorities or fabric routing for QoS.

description: Function-level reset capability is not implemented. The device supports only POR, system reset, and software hot reset. Finer-grained FLR-based recovery is unavailable. Recovery must use broader reset mechanisms. Software must not advertise FLR.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for function-level reset; Section 2.13 Reset Considerations
Remarks: Structure drivers to handle hot reset paths rather than FLR.

feature_name: Not supported: PCIe beacon for in-band wake
description: In-band wake signaling via PCIe beacon is not supported. Platform wake must use alternative mechanisms. This limits certain low-power wake scenarios. Do not depend on beacon-based wake events. Coordinate with system PM for out-of-band wake.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for PCI Express beacon for in-band wake
Remarks: Use GPIO or PME# alternatives for wake signaling as applicable.

description: The controller does not include built-in hardware support for PCIe hot plug. Dynamic insertion/removal must be managed by external logic if needed. Software should not rely on hot-plug controller registers. Designs should assume static links. Any hot-plug behavior requires board-level support.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No built-in hardware support for hot plug
Remarks: Limit deployments to fixed endpoints unless external hot-plug solutions are provided.

description: Vendor-defined PCIe messaging is not supported. Only standard message types are handled. Software must not transmit or expect vendor messages. This simplifies interoperability. It also constrains proprietary control planes over PCIe.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for vendor messaging
Remarks: Use standard messages or sideband mechanisms for proprietary control.

feature_name: Not supported: inbound IO access in RC/EP
description: Inbound IO space accesses are not supported in either RC or EP modes. Only memory and configuration spaces are serviced inbound. Systems must avoid mapping IO space to this device. Any such accesses will be unsupported. This avoids legacy IO decoding complexity.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for IO access in inbound direction in RC or EP mode
Remarks: Use MMIO mappings via BARs for all control/data instead of IO space.

feature_name: Not supported: non-incremental addressing and cacheable space
description: Addressing modes other than incremental for bursts are not supported. Consequently, PCIe addresses must not be placed in cacheable memory space. This constrains how masters target memory. Software must use incremental bursts only. Cacheability attributes must avoid PCIe MMIO regions.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for addressing modes other than incremental; addresses cannot be in cacheable memory space
Remarks: Mark PCIe MMIO as strongly-ordered or device memory in page tables.

feature_name: Not supported: auxiliary power for D3 cold state retention
description: There is no auxiliary power to maintain controller state to exit D3 cold. State is lost in D3 cold and requires full reinitialization. System PM must account for this limitation. Do not plan for warm resume from D3 cold. Use D3 hot or L1 where possible.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No auxiliary power to maintain controller state to come out of D3 cold state
Remarks: Persist any required context in nonvolatile or system memory before D3 cold.

description: The L2 link state is not implemented. Deepest supported link idle state is L1. Power savings associated with L2 are unavailable. System sleep flows must be designed accordingly. Ensure link partners are configured without L2 expectations.
evidence: Section 1.3 Not supported: No support for L2 link state
Remarks: Use L1 substates only if documented; validate resume timing without L2.
